replies.author: @traincarwillie
replies.text:
Think of it like water. An Amp is really 1 coulomb over 1 second. A coulomb is an amount of electrons. 

If I setup a gravity tank, to power a  turbine, a large pipe with a large turbine can use a low pressure(volts) while moving a lot of water(amps) to turn the large turbine slowly. To get the same overall power, you could use a small turbine, with a higher pressure system to turn the small turbine faster. Assuming we want the same overall work output, horsepower(watts) the large turbine  will have high torque but low RPMs, where the small turbine will have high rpms but low torque.

I typically listen using the internal speaker on my phone or an external one but decided to use my earbuds for this one and my goodness man you have a plethora of happy birds around.  I replayed the last couple mins using just the phone speaker and I could make out what you were saying a little better but at times their chatting and singing was matching and/or possibly exceeding your voice audio. Just thought I’d mention it because while I do enjoy a little nature in the background I really come here to learn something new and/or gain a different perspective or additional knowledge about something I already know and if I’m lucky stumble upon something that jump starts and excites my mental gears which happens quite often on this channel.  

While I do love the birds I find that they seem to talk about the same stuff over and over and they have a bad habit of talking over one another so if possible in future videos I’d like to hear you more clearly than them.  😁

author: @smob0
text: One problem is leakage current through the wire casing/lacquer coating increases with voltage. Voltage getting too high could also potentially short coils. A thicker casing would help but then you can't wind as many coils in a given volume. At a certain point you'll probably be better off trading voltage output for current output to reduce the amount of insulator, and the amount of effort you'd need to isolate the voltage until it's switched down.

author: @schitlipz
text: Technically it's coulombs passing an area per unit time.  Doesn't need to be in a circuit.  Doesn't even need to be electrons.

replies.author: @schitlipz
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering That many electrons do carry that much charge, but they do not necessarily need to be the constituents of that charge.

Apologies for splitting hairs, but it's one of those things that seems a little too informal which may get locked into some heads.

For most practical purposes, the description of current as flowing electrons in a circuit is adequate.

I love the stuff you build.  Very cool.

author: @OspreyGozo
text: I made a little Savonius turbine that turns a NEMA17 which, in turn, creates some power after being connected to two bridge rectifiers and a 1000uF smoothing capacitor. It does a good job at lighting some LEDs but the problem is that due to the erratic nature of wind where I live, the turbine spins very fast for like  about 10 seconds and then stops spinning for about 20 seconds. It is not that regular but you get the idea. While this is fine for an LED, I suspect that charging li-ion batteries would not be a good idea. I was wondering whether replacing the smoothing capacitor with a 1F super capacitor would alleviate the problem. I am asking for your opinion before going through the trouble of ordering them (they need to be a set with protection circuitry in order to handle anything more than 2.7 volts).

replies.author: @jima3252
replies.text: You didn't mention the volts or amps that the nema17 makes. And were the bridge rectifiers in series or parallel?

replies.author: @OspreyGozo
replies.text: @jima3252 The bridge rectifiers are set in parallel. The NEMA17 generates around 5V but can go up to 10V in short gusts. With the load I am hoping to operate, the current is around 1.2A at 5V or 460ma at 12V.

author: @wbeaty
text:
Inside our lamp-cords, the electrons wiggle slightly back and forth.  Their motion is measured in amps!    And, in a DC circuit, the electricity flows in a complete circle, like the motion of a flywheel or a moving drive-belt.  That's the amperes.  Amps are a closed-loop flow.    (The electrical energy is completely different, measured in watts, and zooming along at nearly lightspeed.)   
When we realize that two separate things are flowing along wires, suddenly it all starts making sense.    It's very much like air, versus wind, versus sound-waves.  Air is like the electricity stored inside all metals, wind is like the electricity-flow, and sound is like the electrical energy.   (Air is the Coulombs.   Wind is the Amperes.   Sound is the Joules.)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like to think of AC like a swing and DC like a push - it's basically the same thing - acting in a different way

replies.author: @schitlipz
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I like to think of AC like rubbing a rope around a tree, creating a heck of a lot of heat.  But current doesn't necessarily need to be in a circuit.  It's a detail of physics.

replies.author: @wbeaty
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I think the key to the whole concept is this:   wires are pre-filled with electricity, and when their internal electricity starts flowing, we call it current, and measure it in amperes.  (Wires are completely filled with mobile electrons, the "sea of charge.")   
So, if electric circuits are like a long rope, or like a drive-belt, then Amps is measuring the speed of the rope or belt.  AC is easy to understand: just have your rope be wiggling!   It still heats up the shafts on the pullies, same as when its moving constantly.   (Yet most people were wrongly taught that the pipes are empty, the rope is not there, and that electrons move at lightspeed through the "hollow pipes."

author: @michaelsohocki1573
text:
This has been explained to me so many times I'm embarrassed. (I still don't completely get it, but by now this is entirely MY fault.) Sigh. But for academic's sake, here you go:

1. Volts are the buses (schoolbuses in some stories), amps are the workers (students in some stories.

2. volts are the presence, amps are the work, and hertz are the speed

3. electricity is a waterhose: volts are like the diameter of the hose, amps are the pressure

4. electrons are like little men (this is probably Medhi from Electroboom because I can hear his voice in my head as I say this), volts are like the little walking shoulder to shoulder--amps are the little men walking in a line, a bumping the guy in front of them--equivalent to the "shove" that Rob explains. 

Hope that was fun. Cheers. :)

author: @Ferd414
text: I've always preferred the "water" analogy for electricity - Volts equals how much pressure is on the water (and therefore, how fast it's moving), while amps equals how many gallons of  water is going past some point on the course it's traveling - So volts EFFECTIVELY equals the speed the water is moving (say 50MPH, just for illustration) while Amps are "how much water went past that point in any given time period" (say 100 gallons). A conductor that can carry high voltage (speed/pressure) but low amps (gallons) is like a bunch of kids throwing half-liter bottles of water past the point - The bottles go past quick, but the number of gallons that goes by per time-period is small. Conversely, a low voltage, high amperage conductor is like a bunch of guys in semis (lorries to you british folk) loaded with case upon case upon case of bottled water crawling up a steep grade - The water isn't moving very fast, but a WHOLE BUNCH of it is going past in a short amount of time.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @some-other-guy
text:
At some point when i was a kid, my physics teacher said the one short sentence that i needed to start understanding the very basics of electricity: "Voltage is a state, current is an event". 
Much later, when i was installing a fuse panel, a friend reminded me that "high voltage electrocutes you, high current burns down the house". I just needed to share those invaluable quotes.
This video made me think about how to judge the efficiency of any given generator using the 2 edge cases.
idle (no load / open circuit / infinite resistance): would the input work only be impeded by mechancial friction or will there be some significant internal lossy current (turning to heat)?
short ("zero" resistace load): would a theoretical coil that never burns just stop the input work from being done (stopping the generator)?
My thoughts are essentially about where the mechanical energy goes that isn't available as electrical output, and i wonder if these "load" cases might help putting that in perspective.
Oh, and the birds in your garden have the potential to overpower you, but sure generate a nice atmosphere!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @electricAB
text:
Peddling a bicycle works well with your analogy… no chain (circuit) no way to do any “shoving”…
High voltage low amps, turned into lower voltage higher amps is how the grid works.
The transformers are part of the load and part of the losses of the system. 
Which is another reason why local generation for local use is going to be more efficient than centralised generation - depending of course on what we’re doing the “shoving” with and what we need to have “shoved”.

author: @EZEMPIRE_LABORLINKED
text:
Edward's EZ Equilibrium Rotation Law
"In a closed system, rotational equilibrium is achieved when the sum of torques acting on the system about any axis is zero. This principle applies universally across physical systems, maintaining balance and stability in rotational motion."
This law states that in any closed system, the rotational equilibrium is maintained when the total torque (the rotational force) acting on the system around any chosen axis is balanced, ensuring steady rotational motion without acceleration or deceleration.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text:
I have always looked at electricity as water and vice versa. Imagine a thin tube of 1mm inside diameter. It has water at a pressure of 10 bar in it. The plastic material has to be thick and strong to contain that pressure. This high pressure is the equivalent of high voltage which btw also needs to have a thick insulating material to contain the high voltage electrons (think spark plug cables).  But there is not of electrons in this 1mm tube moving at any one time. Then there is the case of a bigger tube of 100mm dia with a low water  pressure of 0,1 bar(think volts). With that low pressure the plastic tube can be very thin material similar to having thin low voltage insulation. But in this case there is a lot of electrons available in that pipe but just not a lot moving past a given point at any one time because of the low voltage (pressure).

The turbines needed for these 2 situations above are different. In the first case the turbine needs to be high revving with small buckets for the electron jet. In case 2 the turbine needs to be big buckets but the turbine will run at slow speed and high torque. In either case there will not be a lot of power produced as such as there is not a lot of electrons with good speed. Now imagine if we had 100mm tube with water at 10bar pressure, now you talking about lots of electrons traveling at high pressure. The turbine for this case will be big and make lots of power. That is how I see volts and amps relating, you need both for any useful work to be done.

author: @ericwazhung
text:
As an EE, it's sometimes hard to explain concepts like these to folk who don't have experience of their own.  I appreciate your taking time to explain this concept in a way many folk will understand!

I also, especially, appreciate your clearly pointing out that your *personal tendency* is to feel like more volts means more power. And why. (the ability to transform voltages).
I've seen many of your videos and always feel (my own personal tendency to feel) that the voltage-measurements you get from various generators are impressive, but (I'm sorry, it's my feeling) a bit meaningless without a load to show the actual *power* generated.
BUT: That is a tough topic, since the only way to measure power is to attach a load, and, in general, the maximum amount of power that such a generator (or solar cell, etc.) can produce is highly-dependent, as you said, on a load being *matched* to the generator.
So, it creates a chicken-egg problem, that you've had me pondering for some time... How do we find the [maximum] power-output of our power sources, the easiest way possible? And, from there, how do we match our load to that source?

Solar panels are very interesting (to me) examples. A single solar cell is essentially a diode that creates current proportional to the amount of light on it. That *sounds* obvious, but it's deeper than one might think. And your explanation of amps/shove, here, tiuches on *why.*
See, if there's no place for that current to go, then it's just a voltage-source. Kind of like a motor-based generator with no load. But: because soloar cells are *diodes*, they start *conducting* at around 0.6V. So they really only act like motor-generators at *less than* 0.6V. At 0.6V they act like a motor-generator that has a diode across its terminals; as a load that turns-on at 0.6V, and decreases its resistance as the generator increses its output power... Thus, maintaining roughly 0.6V at its terminals regardless of the available power... and *wasting* any power that results form higher than 0.6V in the form of heat through the diode! (and too much heat-produced by/in that diode can damage it!)
It's a weird concept to consider. [Maximim] Power-matching, for solar cells, is important-enough that it's considered quite a lot more than for other power-sources.
I'm not certain, but my guess is that a solar cell is best-off if its output voltage is kept below 0.5V (lower than rated), and I hear their outputs can even be shorted, safely, so any power generated results in heat in the shorting wire rather than in the cell itself!
So, now we have a weird scenario where voltage-output is not at all an indication of available power... A hypothetical large-enough cell could produce AMPS at 0.5V, many watts. A panel of such cells, 100 in series, might output 60V. At low-light it might only maintain 60V at 100mA (6W), but at high light it might produce 0.5V on each cell, for 50V, with a [different] 5A load (250W!) OTOH, if the load were matched differently, those same lighting conditions might result in 40V with a 7A load (280W). BUT, that doesn't imply more power by merely dropping the output voltage. If it were dragged down to 10V (instead of its rated 60V) it might produce more amps 10A (100W) but now we've got much less than the maximum power (upwards of 280W in this hypothetical scenario).

I, personally, find this somewhat fascinating... And, I know it's rather complicated, but understanding this about solar cells has somehow helped me to understand power-matching in general; as, aside from the diode, most generators have similar phenomenon. 

E.G. as you pointed out, the same amount of water might flow into the turbine of a hydro, but if the hydro's load is too heavy, it will just stall the shaft. If it's too light, then the hydro's output-voltage will remain relatively constant, but basically all the power it *could* be putting out goes unused. (but, unlike a solar cell, it isn't wasted as heat).

So, power-matching is an interesting topic. E.G. if one's using an intermittent power-source (like solar or wind) to charge a battery, it'd be ideal to charge that battery at the maximum rate possible with the available wind/sunlight. And, even more ideally, to do-so at the maximum rate (by drawing the maximum power available) as the wind changes and sunlight varies with clouds. 

THAT is a complicated challenge, because, again, the only way to know the actual load necessary to match the source means constantly measuring the voltage and current (multiplying them for watts), while *varying* the load! Who has a variable load like that?

So, they've developed "MPPT" devices, with onboard computers, and variable input/output DC-to-DC converters that do exactly that. Though, I'm sure they come in various forms and quality-grades.

If you're connecting your generators to "the grid", I guess, ideally, you'd use one of these to always/constantly transform your maximum power-available to the grid voltage. OTOH, if you're only charging batteries, then what do you do with that maximum-power-available when the batteries are full?

And, again, such MPPT devices usually involve a (small) computer, and DC converters that, frankly, couldn't've existed a few decades ago, nevermind be designed/built (or maybe even be understood/configured) by even advanced hobbiests/experimenters

So, it's an ongoing thought: How to make these concepts more accessible... especially to folk experimenting with generators of the sorts you experiment with. Is there a simple device/method for measuring *power* output by these devices? Sure, tie a resistor to the output and measure the voltage across it, use ohm's law, calculate power. But, again, for most of these devices the maximum amount of power they can output may vary dramatically with the amount of load-resistance chosen.
Maybe, at the simplest, grab two $10 multimeters, and a high-wattage potentiometer... turn it through its range until volts*amps is at its max? Maybe there's a cheap watt-meter available that can work with a wide range of voltages? Or cheap multimeters with USB outputs that could be connected to a computer to do the math?

I dunno, my tendency is to build such things when I need them...

But, of course, measurement of available power is just the start... "Transforming" the available voltage/amperage to the needs of a load (matching the source to the load) is another step. Possibly requiring a DC-DC buck, or even boot converter.

Full-on MPPT is a bit much for an experimenter. But, turning a potentiometer and watching a meter is doable, to get a sense of what sorts of loads *could* be attached to the generators you/they come up with, and what sort of (DC-to-DC?) converter would help.

replies.text: The simplistic way, as I understand it, is to match the internal resistance of the generator to the resistance of the load, for maximum power transfer. So a 10 ohm coil in the generator and a 10 ohm load, for example.

author: @jondelaire
text: Electrons move extremely slow. Much slower than the fields that make up the electric current. The amount of magnetism in a circuit is measured in Amps, the amount of the dielectric field is measured in volts. When these two fields work in conjunction you get watts.

author: @donaldhoudek2889
text:
The way I remember these important things is:

Amps is the flow rate of energy, like fuel flow.
Volts is like the fuel pressure
kW is like current speed
kWh is like Miles per Gallon 

Good video!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Thanks for sharing

replies.author: @wbeaty
replies.text:
But Watts are the flow-rate of the energy.  Not amps.  Two separate things are flowing in circuits, electrons and energy.  Electrons flow slowly, going in a circle.  Energy flows at lightspeed, going one way (going from the source to the load.)

Analogies: Amperes are like the motion of a drive-belt, or like the speed of gears.  Or, in a hydraulic system, amps are like the flow of the oil.

Think of it this way:  in AC systems, the energy zips along at lightspeed.  But the electrons just sit there and wiggle slightly.  The electrons' AC motion is measured in amperes.

Or, if we suddenly yank on a long rope, the rope-wave goes zooming off to the far end.  That's the energy.   But the rope itself moves towards you, and it also moves quite slowly.  That's like amps.

Or, if you yell loudly, the air wiggles back and forth.  That's like the amps.   But at the same time, the sound-wave goes zooming outwards at hundreds of KPH.  That's energy, and energy-flow is measured in watts.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @wbeaty errr - energy doesn't flow per se - it's transferred from one point to another - the electron is the medium of transfer - The watt is a measure of the rate of energy transfer over a unit of time, note - transfer.

replies.author: @wbeaty

author: @401ksolar
text:
Just had this conversation with a neighbor today, he grasped the concept when I stated that higher resistance will "impede" the amperage and lower resistance will "encourage"  the amperage.

A nichrome wire in a heater or incandescent light bulb has lower resistance (fewer ohms) than a semiconductor like a light emitting diode (led) + current limiting resistor. 
And that is why incandescent uses more power (p=iv) lower resistance higher amps.

author: @robertbooth7396
text:
I started to understand amps when I started to think of amps being what appliances in the circuit “suck” and volts are what the source pushes. That doesn’t answer every question on the difference between volts and amps but it helped me when I was wiring a 240 volt line into my work shop. 
I always appreciate your educational YouTubes, Robert. Thank you for taking the time to produce this. Amazing to me, better than 8 minutes talking on a rather complicated and confusing subject in a very clear cogent and uncomplicated manner with many excellent metaphors for the concepts, and not a single cut or edit. 
Cheers, my friend!
oddjobbob

replies.author: @MostlyPennyCat
replies.text:
The water analogy often works well to an extent. 
Potential/Voltage is water pressure. 
A pressure difference and the resistance of the connecting pipe defined the water speed, which is Current/Amps.
Resistance/Ohms can be a grid across the connecting pipe, it maintains a potential difference across itself, higher to lower Voltage, converting that difference into heat.
For capacitors, you have to add an imaginary field around water that affects water to water.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I get it mate - but 'suck' is kind of about face view - sure they 'suck' at the wall outlet - but somewhere something is 'pushing'

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text:
One comparison which is easy for me to remember is speed (volts) and thickness (amps). So the higher the voltage, the longer the spark, like running faster means jumping further,  and the thicker the rope, the stronger it is. 
Another analogy is car traffic on a bridge. If you have one lane (e.g. 1 amp), the speed of the cars (volts) defines how many cars (watts) cross the bridge per hour. If you want more cars crossing per hour you can increase either the speed or the number of lanes. More lanes require a wider bridge, so more amps require thicker wires. The analogy could be extended to include resistance,magnetic fields and other properties of a circuit, but this covers the basics.
